// JV002 (chain, port of the F5 object-flow principle): a new exception thrown in
// a catch must carry the caught as its cause - as a constructor argument, or by
// attaching it with initCause / addSuppressed. The chain breaks, and JV002 fires,
// only when the caught reaches the new exception solely through a string
// (getMessage / toString / string concatenation) or does not reach it at all.
// JV001 is orthogonal: any throw makes the catch non-silent, so these are clean
// for JV001.
class Jv002Chain {
    void droppedCause() {
        try {
            risky();
        } catch (Exception e) {
            throw new RuntimeException("boom"); // want "JV002"
        }
    }

    void causeAsSoleArg() {
        try {
            risky();
        } catch (Exception e) {
            throw new RuntimeException(e);
        }
    }

    void causeAsSecondArg() {
        try {
            risky();
        } catch (Exception e) {
            throw new IllegalStateException("boom", e);
        }
    }

    void stringifiedMessage() {
        try {
            risky();
        } catch (Exception e) {
            throw new RuntimeException(e.getMessage()); // want "JV002"
        }
    }

    // String.valueOf is a stringifier too, same as getMessage/toString.
    void stringValueOf() {
        try {
            risky();
        } catch (Exception e) {
            throw new RuntimeException(String.valueOf(e)); // want "JV002"
        }
    }

    // Exact mixed form: message from the caught plus the caught itself as
    // cause - clean, the object still reaches the new exception.
    void messageAndCauseMixed() {
        try {
            risky();
        } catch (Exception e) {
            throw new RuntimeException(e.getMessage(), e);
        }
    }

    // Adversarial: the caught is textually present but only through string
    // concatenation, so the constructed cause is null and the chain is lost.
    void stringConcatBreaks() {
        try {
            risky();
        } catch (Exception e) {
            throw new IllegalStateException("ctx: " + e); // want "JV002"
        }
    }

    // Stringified in the message yet still carried as the object cause: clean.
    void stringifiedButCarried() {
        try {
            risky();
        } catch (Exception e) {
            throw new IllegalStateException("ctx: " + e.getMessage(), e);
        }
    }

    void twoStepInitCause() {
        try {
            risky();
        } catch (Exception e) {
            RuntimeException wrapped = new RuntimeException("boom");
            wrapped.initCause(e);
            throw wrapped;
        }
    }

    void twoStepAddSuppressed() {
        try {
            risky();
        } catch (Exception e) {
            RuntimeException wrapped = new RuntimeException("boom");
            wrapped.addSuppressed(e);
            throw wrapped;
        }
    }

    // initCause is called on a DIFFERENT local than the one thrown - the
    // thrown exception still drops the cause.
    void twoStepInitCauseWrongVariable() {
        try {
            risky();
        } catch (Exception e) {
            RuntimeException wrapped = new RuntimeException("boom");
            RuntimeException other = new RuntimeException("other");
            other.initCause(e);
            throw wrapped; // want "JV002"
        }
    }

    // Two-step that drops the cause: the local is a bare new exception and
    // nothing attaches the caught before it is thrown.
    void twoStepDropsCause() {
        try {
            risky();
        } catch (Exception e) {
            RuntimeException wrapped = new RuntimeException("boom");
            throw wrapped; // want "JV002"
        }
    }

    // Reassignment: the declaration drops the cause but the last binding
    // (a plain AssignExpr, not a VariableDeclarator) carries it. The LAST
    // assignment wins, matching go astutil's localAssignRHS.
    void reassignedAddsCause() {
        try {
            risky();
        } catch (Exception e) {
            RuntimeException wrapped = new RuntimeException("boom");
            wrapped = new RuntimeException("wrap", e);
            throw wrapped;
        }
    }

    // Reassignment the other direction: the declaration carries the cause but
    // the last binding drops it - JV002 must fire on the LAST binding's shape.
    void reassignedDropsCause() {
        try {
            risky();
        } catch (Exception e) {
            RuntimeException wrapped = new RuntimeException("wrap", e);
            wrapped = new RuntimeException("boom");
            throw wrapped; // want "JV002"
        }
    }

    // A reassignment to a factory call (not a `new`) is unknown - the binding
    // is dropped rather than trusted stale, so the throw fails open.
    void reassignedToFactoryCallFailsOpen() {
        try {
            risky();
        } catch (Exception e) {
            RuntimeException wrapped = new RuntimeException("wrap", e);
            wrapped = makeWrapper();
            throw wrapped;
        }
    }

    void risky() throws Exception {}

    RuntimeException makeWrapper() {
        return new RuntimeException("factory");
    }
}
